findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-clone store-initialization step to `run_e2_benchmark.py`: after `git clone --local --branch tickets`, call `rebar ensure-registry` (or equivalent `_gen_local_files`/`rebar init`) against `TMP_MUTANT_STORE` to create `.env-id` before any `rebar edit` injection — mirroring the `reviewbot-ensure-tickets.sh` pattern that resolves this exact failure (composer.py:400 gates all writes on `.env-id` presence; fresh clones never carry it).
evidence: Plan: 'the harness therefore creates the mutant store with `git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>`'
Plan: 'injects each seeded defect into the clone via `rebar edit <id> --description=…`'
src/rebar/_commands/composer.py:400: `if not (tracker / '.env-id').is_file(): raise CommandError('Error: ticket system not initialized. Run ticket init first.')`
src/rebar/_commands/init.py:40: `_GITIGNORE = f""".env-id\n...` — .env-id is git-ignored and never committed

tests/unit/test_reviewbot_ensure_tickets.py:118-125: test_fresh_single_branch_clone_write_fails — 'RED state: a fresh clone lacks .env-id (git-ignored), so a store write fails ticket system not initialized'
infra/scripts/reviewbot-ensure-tickets.sh:9-13: documents the same failure mode for fresh clones used in production
findings.finding: The plan's mutant-store isolation sequence — `git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO> <TMP>` followed immediately by `rebar edit <id> --description=…` — will fail because `.env-id` is git-ignored and therefore absent from any fresh clone. Every `rebar edit` call gates on `(tracker / '.env-id').is_file()` (composer.py:400) and raises 'ticket system not initialized' when the file is missing. This is a codebase-confirmed capability gap: `test_reviewbot_ensure_tickets.py:118-125` explicitly tests and documents this exact failure mode ('a fresh clone lacks .env-id (git-ignored), so a store write fails ticket system not initialized'). The plan provides no post-clone initialization step (e.g., `rebar init`, `rebar ensure-registry`, or manual `.env-id` generation via `_gen_local_files`).
findings.location: ## What — 'Gate-execution mechanism' / 'Mutant store = an INDEPENDENT git clone' paragraph; Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; AC7 (ISOLATION-OK prove command)
scenarios: Harness runs `git clone --local --branch tickets REAL_REPO TMP`, then calls `rebar edit <id> --description=<mutated_text>` with `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=TMP`; `rebar edit` finds no `.env-id` in TMP and raises 'ticket system not initialized'; every mutation injection fails; the benchmark cannot run.
The `--self-check-isolation` AC (`run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`) also calls `rebar edit` as part of its single-pair run, hitting the same failure before any gate run occurs.
findings.suggested_fix: After the `git clone --local --branch tickets` call, add: `subprocess.run(['python', '-m', 'rebar', 'ensure-registry', '--tracker', TMP_MUTANT_STORE], check=True)` or directly call `rebar._commands.init._gen_local_files(str(TMP_MUTANT_STORE))` to write `.env-id` (and `.signing-key`) into the cloned store before any `rebar edit` call. Also add a pre-condition assertion `assert (Path(TMP_MUTANT_STORE) / '.env-id').is_file()` after initialization and before the first injection.

findings.finding: The AC7 proving command uses semicolons to separate all statements, so a non-zero exit from `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation` does not stop execution; the HEAD-comparison still runs and prints ISOLATION-OK as long as HEAD was not changed — which it won't be if the script aborted early. The isolation self-check failure is silently swallowed.
findings.location: AC7 proving command (shell snippet)
findings.suggested_fix:
```bash
H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD) && \
.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation && \
H1=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD) && \
[ "$H0" = "$H1" ] && echo ISOLATION-OK
```

findings.finding: The crash-resumable behavior of `run_e2_benchmark.py` ('journals raw verdicts incrementally (`e2_verdicts.jsonl`, crash-resumable)') is a described deliverable in Scope with no covering AC. No prove-command or test asserts that a partial `e2_verdicts.jsonl` allows the harness to skip already-completed rows on re-run.
findings.location: Scope: `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; AC4, AC5, AC6, AC7 (none covers crash-resume)

findings.finding: The plan describes `det_floor.py:345,392` as 'the DET-floor absence probe' that 'makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real.' Reading the file: line 345 is `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'file', 'name': ref}, repo_root=ctx.repo_root)` (P2 file-path resolution — checks whether a file a plan REFERENCES exists) and line 392 is `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'dependency', 'name': pkg}, ...)` (P3 package existence). Neither performs absent-capability probing or unnecessary-mechanism detection. Class-1 (absent asserted capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) are LLM-driven via E4, G1G2, G6, and the agentic verifier — not these DET-floor file/package probes.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: 'the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) … makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real'

findings.finding: No AC verifies that the gate actually received and processed the MUTATED plan text (i.e., that `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` routing to the clone is functioning). The isolation AC (AC 7) checks only that the real store's HEAD is unchanged after a run — it does not confirm that the mutant's modified description was the input to `review_plan`. A broken routing (e.g., `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` silently ignored, gate falling back to real store) would produce systematically low detection rates indistinguishable from 'the gate legitimately cannot catch this class,' silently producing an invalid baseline without triggering any AC failure.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 and AC 7
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s `run_validity` logic (or as a pre-flight check in `--self-check-isolation`): select the class-2 or class-3 smoke-test pair whose baseline detection is expected 'partial' (not near-zero), run it, and assert `mutant_verdict != control_verdict`. Record this check in `e2_run_validity.json` as `{routing_smoke_test: {mutant_verdict, control_verdict, routing_confirmed: bool}}`. Fail the run non-zero if `routing_confirmed` is False.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the count-vs-rate ambiguity for degraded classes: add a `detection_type: 'count' | 'rate'` field to `e2_thresholds.json` (or always store rates 0.0–1.0), and update the AC3 prove command to verify this field exists and the harness `emit_baseline` to apply the appropriate comparison.
evidence: Degradation ladder: 'keep the thresholds as RATES (≥70% detection, ≤10% blocking-FP)'
Threshold table: Class 2 '≥7/10 flagged', Class 5 '≥7/10 flagged' (count notation)
AC3 prove: `assert all({'detection_min','blocking_fp_max'}<=set(t['classes'][k]) for k in t['classes'])` — no `detection_type` or `is_rate` field
If N=5 and detection_min=7 (count), the criterion is logically impossible; if detection_min=0.70 (rate), the harness must divide rather than compare
findings.finding: The degradation ladder (## What, 'Degradation if a class cannot reach 10 real base plans') specifies that when N<10 the thresholds become RATES ('≥70% detection, ≤10% blocking-FP'), but the pre-registered `e2_thresholds.json` format stores only `detection_min` and `blocking_fp_max` per class (AC3 prove command), with no field to distinguish whether the stored value is a count (7) or a rate (0.70). When class 5 (or another class) degrades to N=5–9, a `detection_min: 7` (count) is unachievable at N=5 yet `detection_min: 0.70` (rate) is fine — the implementer has no specification for which representation to use, creating an irreconcilable contradiction between the threshold format and the degradation arithmetic.
findings.location: ## What — 'Degradation if a class cannot reach 10 real base plans' vs. 'Pre-registered per-class thresholds' table vs. AC3 prove command (`e2_thresholds.json` schema)
scenarios: Class 5 degrades to N=7 (within the range); the harness reads detection_min=7 and computes 5/7=71% as 'not meeting threshold 7' — spurious FAIL
Alternatively the harness treats 0.70 as a count and compares 0/5 >= 0.70 — nonsensical
findings.suggested_fix: Store all thresholds as rates (0.0–1.0) in `e2_thresholds.json` (e.g. `detection_min: 0.7`, `blocking_fp_max: 0.1`) and update the AC3 prove command to check `isinstance(t['classes'][k]['detection_min'], float)`. The harness then always computes `detected / n_mutants >= detection_min` regardless of N, eliminating the count/rate split.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct `passes.py:269,514` citation to the actual agentic finder entry point (e.g. `pass1_chunk` at line 281, or the `ProductionBatchRunner` dispatch path) so that the gate-execution mechanism description accurately locates where code-grounding finders run.
evidence: Plan text: 'the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules'
passes.py line 269: inside `_resolve_system()` — `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)`
passes.py line 514: inside `summarize_for_isf()` — `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)`
Actual agentic finders: `pass1_chunk()` (line 281) with `agentic=True`, dispatched via `ProductionBatchRunner` / `RunnerAgentStep`
findings.finding: The plan cites `passes.py:269,514` as the location of 'the agentic finders' that 'read the REAL modules.' Line 269 of passes.py is inside `_resolve_system()` (a prompt-resolution helper that calls `prompts.get_prompt`) and line 514 is inside `summarize_for_isf()` (an ISF log summarizer). Neither is an 'agentic finder' — the actual agentic code-grounding path runs through `ProductionBatchRunner` / `RunnerAgentStep` in the workflow engine. An implementer reading these line citations to understand where to instrument or debug code-grounding in a test run would be misled.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section, `passes.py:269,514` citation
scenarios: An implementer debugging why class-1 (absent capability) detection is not grounding against the real code checks passes.py:269 and 514 and finds only prompt resolution, not agentic tool calls, leading them to the wrong layer.
A reviewer checking whether the mechanism citation is trustworthy finds the line numbers don't match the claimed content, eroding confidence in the gate-execution mechanism section.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `passes.py:269,514` with a reference to `pass1_chunk` (passes.py:281, `agentic=True` path) and/or `production_batch_runner.py` (the `RunnerAgentStep` dispatch), which are the actual agentic finder entry points.

findings.finding: The plan asserts '`force=True` (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh so every mutant runs fresh)' citing `__init__.py:508-518`. But at line 517, drift-refresh is gated by `if sign:` (no `not force` guard), meaning `force=True` alone does NOT bypass drift-refresh. The plan's own mechanism uses `sign=False`, which does bypass drift-refresh (because `if sign:` is False). The inline code comment at line 507 says 'force bypasses BOTH' but the code contradicts this for `sign=True, force=True`. The plan's practical outcome is correct (sign=False skips drift-refresh), but the stated reason ('force=True bypasses drift-refresh') is technically wrong and could cause a future caller using `sign=True, force=True` to believe drift-refresh is bypassed when it is not.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism: 'Non-mutating flags: `sign=False` (no attestation), `emit_sidecar=False` (no `REVIEW_RESULT` write — critical so E2 does not pollute E1/R7's sidecar corpus), `force=True` (skip idempotent-reuse / drift-refresh so every mutant runs fresh) — `__init__.py:508-518,570-587`'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Non-mutating flags note to: '`sign=False` (skips signing, idempotent-reuse, and drift-refresh — all three gated on `sign`), `emit_sidecar=False` (no REVIEW_RESULT write), `force=True` (additionally prevents idempotent-reuse when sign=True, defensive belt-and-suspenders) — `__init__.py:508,517,570`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavior of `--self-check-isolation` precisely: state whether it must execute a full single-pair gate evaluation (and how the pair is selected — e.g., first row of `e2_mutant_corpus.jsonl`, or a hardcoded synthetic pair) in addition to asserting `.git` is a directory, so the HEAD-unchanged check genuinely proves isolation through a real gate run rather than trivially.
evidence: Plan: '`--self-check-isolation` ASSERTS `os.path.isdir(<TMP_MUTANT_STORE>/.git)` before any `rebar edit` injection'
Plan: 'Prove BOTH the standalone-clone assertion and that HEAD is identical before/after a single-pair run'
If the flag is only a structural assertion, the HEAD-unchanged shell check passes trivially without any gate run ever touching the store — the isolation property under a real run is never exercised.
findings.finding: The `--self-check-isolation` flag's runtime behavior is underspecified: the plan says it 'ASSERTS `os.path.isdir(<TMP_MUTANT_STORE>/.git)` before any `rebar edit` injection', but the AC7 proving command also expects the HEAD-unchanged check to prove isolation *through a real single-pair gate run*. If `--self-check-isolation` only asserts the `.git` directory structure without actually running a gate evaluation on one pair, the HEAD-unchanged portion of the proof trivially passes (nothing ran) — a proxy that bypasses the behavior being tested. The executor cannot determine whether to implement `--self-check-isolation` as (a) a structural assertion only or (b) a full single-pair gate run plus structural assertion.
findings.location: AC: 'The benchmark run is ISOLATED from the shared store...' / Scope: `run_e2_benchmark.py` / proving command: `H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); ... --self-check-isolation; H1=...`
scenarios: Executor implements `--self-check-isolation` as only a `.git`-dir check; the HEAD-unchanged assertion in the AC passes trivially; a future gate run that accidentally commits to the real store would not have been caught by this AC.
Executor implements it as a full single-pair run but must invent which pair to run — no specification of pair selection logic exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the scope description of `run_e2_benchmark.py`: '`--self-check-isolation`: asserts `os.path.isdir(<TMP_MUTANT_STORE>/.git)` on the clone, then runs the gate on the FIRST row of `runs/e2_mutant_corpus.jsonl` (or a synthetic pair if the corpus is not yet built), then asserts the real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged; exits 0 on success, non-zero on any failure.' This makes the HEAD check non-trivial and ties the pair selection to a deterministic rule.

findings.finding: The threshold table in ## What pins class 5's detection target as '≥7/10 flagged' (an absolute count requiring N=10 mutants), but the class-5 degradation ladder in the same section explicitly allows class 5 to run at N≥5 with 'rate' thresholds (≥70%), and AC #1's prove command only asserts m['5']>=5. At N=5, the count threshold '≥7/10' is literally unachievable, creating a direct contradiction between the threshold table and the degradation ladder that would leave an implementer unable to determine whether a class-5 run at N=7 with 5 detections passes (5/7 ≈ 71% ≥ 70% rate, but 5 < 7 absolute count).
findings.location: ## What — Pre-registered per-class thresholds table (class 5 row) vs. Degradation ladder subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Change the threshold table class-5 row from '≥7/10 flagged' to '≥70% flagged (≥7/10 at N=10; rate applies when N<10)' and add a `rate_fallback: true` field to class 5's entry in e2_thresholds.json, tested in the AC #3 structure check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `target_criteria` field per class to `e2_thresholds.json` (e.g. `{'1': {'target_criteria': ['E4','G1G2','G6'], ...}, '5': {'target_criteria': [], ...}}`) and update AC3's prove command to verify this field is present for all 5 classes, ensuring `classify_pair` uses the spec-defined criteria set at runtime.
evidence: Counting rule: 'a pure helper `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)`'
Class-5 table row: 'Current-gate nearest criteria (baseline): none'
AC3 prove command checks only `detection_min`, `blocking_fp_max`, `indeterminate_cap` — no `target_criteria` field
No section specifies where `target_criteria` per class is stored or how the harness obtains it at runtime
findings.finding: The `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` function requires a `target_criteria` list for each class (## What counting-rule section), but the plan never specifies what `target_criteria` value is used for class 5 in the E2 baseline run. The threshold table explicitly says class 5 has 'none' as current-gate nearest criteria; the harness must supply some value to `classify_pair` for class 5. An empty set `[]` would produce baseline ≈ 0 (correct), but the plan leaves this unspecified — and neither `e2_thresholds.json` (AC3) nor the harness spec (## Scope) names a `target_criteria` field or source for any class.
findings.location: ## What — counting rule; class-5 row in the baseline table; AC3 prove command; Scope `e2_counting.py` and `run_e2_benchmark.py`
scenarios: Implementer must decide: hard-code target_criteria per class in the harness? store in e2_thresholds.json? The plan's AC3 doesn't verify a target_criteria field exists, so a reviewer cannot confirm the correct value was used
For class 5 specifically, if target_criteria=['p6_ac_quality'] is used (incorrect — plan says it's not a detector), the baseline would be nonzero and misleadingly suggest current detection
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"target_criteria": ["E4", "G1G2", "G6"]` (classes 1/4), `["G3", "G4"]` (classes 2/3), `[]` (class 5) to each class entry in `e2_thresholds.json`, mirroring the 'Current-gate nearest criteria' table column. Update AC3 to assert `'target_criteria' in t['classes'][k]` for all k.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `assemble_e2_mutants.py`'s class-2/3 mutation procedure whether child titles are also mutated (e.g., renamed to reflect the layer/artifact pattern) alongside descriptions, and add a construct-validity check in the AC proving command that asserts class-2 mutants carry at least one child whose title contains a layer keyword (schema|backend|frontend|test).
evidence: Plan says 'injects each seeded defect into the clone via `rebar edit <id> --description=…` (and child edits for classes 2/3)' — only --description is named
Class 2 mutation definition: 'children re-partitioned by architectural layer (schema/backend/frontend/tests)' — partitioning requires renaming children, not just redescribing them
gate_dispatch.py:141 comment: pass-1 criteria read full ticket state including title; pass1.py builds plan_text from both title and description
The plan says the mutation is 'mechanical' but does not specify whether child --title edits are included alongside --description for classes 2/3
findings.finding: The class-2 (layer-cake) and class-3 (consumed-artifact / ordering) mutation mechanism specifies only `rebar edit <id> --description=…` for child injection, but a convincing layer-cake split also requires mutating child TITLES. Real children in cleanly-closed plans have vertical-slice titles (e.g. 'Ship user auth flow'); if titles are left intact while only descriptions are edited to say 'schema layer / frontend layer', the gate's LLM pass-1 container criteria read both titles and descriptions and may correctly identify the tickets as vertical slices despite the description mutation, making the class-2 mutants unconvincing and inflating FN rates artifactually rather than measuring genuine detector gaps.
findings.location: ## What — class 2 and 3 mutation definitions; Scope — assemble_e2_mutants.py
scenarios: A class-2 base plan has children titled 'Implement auth', 'Add tests for auth', 'Deploy auth' — after description-only edits to say 'schema layer', 'backend layer', 'frontend layer', the LLM sees conflicting title/description signals and may produce lower-confidence layer-cake findings, reducing the baseline detection rate in a way that is an artifact of mutation fidelity, not genuine detector performance

A class-3 mutant edits a child description to name a sibling's artifact while the child title already suggests ordering — the gate's container criteria in pass1.py see the title-level sequencing and may still fire, blurring the baseline detection attributed to the ordering edge removal
findings.suggested_fix: For class-2 mutations, have `assemble_e2_mutants.py` use both `rebar edit <child_id> --title='Schema layer: …' --description='…'` (or equivalent in-memory mutation before writing the corpus) so the mutant's child titles are consistent with the seeded defect. The proving AC should verify the mutation_diff includes title changes. For class-3, the title may stay intact (the defect is the missing dependency edge, not the child name), so only describe class-2 as requiring title mutation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `target_criteria` list per class to the `e2_thresholds.json` schema (and update AC 3's prove command to assert it), making the class→criteria attribution mapping a first-class pre-registered artifact alongside `detection_min` and `blocking_fp_max`.
evidence: Plan says: 'A pure helper classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria) applies the rules below' — but never defines what target_criteria IS for each class at call-site.
The baseline table lists 'nearest criteria' (e.g. E4, G1G2, G6 for class 1) but only as an informal description, not as a normative definition of target_criteria.
AC 3 prove command: `assert set(t['classes'])=={'1','2','3','4','5'} and all({'detection_min','blocking_fp_max'}<=set(t['classes'][k])` — no `target_criteria` key required in e2_thresholds.json.

AC 5 says the golden test must pin 'a verdict dict {"advisory": [{"criteria": ["E4","G6"], ...}], ...} against target_criteria=["E4"]' — but never says where class→target_criteria mapping is authoritatively stored for the harness to read at runtime.
findings.finding: The `target_criteria` per-class value vocabulary is referenced throughout the plan (in `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)`, in the counting-rule examples, and in the baseline table's 'nearest criteria' column) but is never formally defined as a canonical artifact. The `e2_thresholds.json` schema (proven by AC 3's `assert all({'detection_min','blocking_fp_max'}<=set(t['classes'][k])...)`) contains no `target_criteria` field, and the plan never specifies whether these values live in `e2_thresholds.json`, are hardcoded in `e2_counting.py`, or are passed from the harness — leaving `classify_pair`'s second argument undefined at call-site.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE verdict handling; Scope — e2_counting.py; AC 3 (e2_thresholds.json prove command); AC 5 (classify_pair golden tests)
scenarios: An implementer writing run_e2_benchmark.py must choose where to look up target_criteria per class — hardcode in e2_counting.py vs. read from e2_thresholds.json vs. inline in the harness — with no normative answer from the plan.

If target_criteria is hardcoded in e2_counting.py but not in e2_thresholds.json, the pre-registration commit containing e2_thresholds.json does not fully pre-register the attribution logic, weakening the pre-registration guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `e2_thresholds.json` with a `target_criteria` array per class, e.g. `{"classes": {"1": {"detection_min": 0.8, "blocking_fp_max": 0.1, "target_criteria": ["E4","G1G2","G6"]}, ...}}`. Update AC 3's assert to include `'target_criteria' in t['classes'][k]`. The harness reads `target_criteria` from this file; `e2_counting.py` accepts it as a parameter only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify precisely in the plan (and mirror in the `e2_counting.run_validity` docstring / AC 6 fixture description) whether `indeterminate_frac` counts rows where `verdict['verdict']=='INDETERMINATE'` only, rows with any finding in `verdict['indeterminate']`, or the union — and update the AC 6 fixture to exercise whichever case is correct.
evidence: Plan: 'run_validity(per_class_verdicts, indeterminate_cap=0.20) computes, per class, the INDETERMINATE fraction over mutants+controls and valid = frac <= cap' — no definition of what constitutes 'INDETERMINATE' for this fraction.
orchestrator.py:501: `verdict = 'INDETERMINATE' if _any_blocking_criterion(indeterminate) else 'PASS'` — a PASS verdict can still have findings in `verdict['indeterminate']`.

The counting rule for classify_pair says 'an INDETERMINATE verdict (or a target-family finding with decision==indeterminate) counts as NOT DETECTED' — the 'or' case (finding-level) is distinct from the verdict-level case.
The AC 6 test fixture only checks '>20%-INDETERMINATE fixture' without specifying whether this is 20% of rows with verdict==INDETERMINATE or 20% of rows with any indeterminate findings.
findings.finding: The `run_validity` helper's `indeterminate_frac` computation is ambiguous between verdict-level INDETERMINATE (where `verdict['verdict'] == 'INDETERMINATE'`) and finding-level indeterminate (where `len(verdict['indeterminate']) > 0` on a PASS verdict). The real gate can return `verdict='PASS'` with a non-empty `indeterminate` findings list (confirmed: `finalize_verdict` at `orchestrator.py:501-505` can return PASS when `_any_blocking_criterion(indeterminate)` is False). The plan says `run_validity` 'computes, per class, the INDETERMINATE fraction over mutants+controls' without specifying which of the two forms to count.
findings.location: ## What — Run-validity guard (enforced, not advisory); Scope — e2_counting.py run_validity
scenarios: Implementer counts only rows where verdict=='INDETERMINATE' — misses PASS verdicts with non-empty indeterminate findings, underreporting the indeterminate fraction and allowing a degenerate run to pass validity.
Implementer counts any row with indeterminate findings — over-reports, flagging otherwise-valid runs.

evidence: description is 29855 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement the crash-resumption deduplication rule in `run_e2_benchmark.py`: on startup, load already-processed `pair_id` values from `e2_verdicts.jsonl` (if it exists) and skip those pairs; document this in the ## Scope section and in the AC's proving command (e.g., assert unique pair_ids in the verdicts file).
evidence: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' — asserts the property but provides no mechanism
The `emit_baseline` AC proves by structure: `[json.loads(l)['verdict'] for l in open('runs/e2_verdicts.jsonl')]` — iterates ALL lines; duplicates would be counted twice
No mention of set-membership check on pair_id, line-counting skip logic, or file-truncation-before-restart strategy
findings.finding: The crash-resumability claim ('crash-resumable' via incremental journaling to `e2_verdicts.jsonl`) is not backed by a specified deduplication/skip mechanism: the plan does not say whether re-runs check existing `pair_id` entries before re-invoking the gate, so a re-run after partial completion could append duplicate verdict rows, causing `emit_baseline` to double-count detections and produce an invalid baseline without any error.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py`; AC: 'writes `runs/e2_run_validity.json` on EVERY run … ONLY when every class passed the validity guard'
scenarios: Harness runs 60/100 rows, crashes; user re-runs; harness appends rows 1–60 again; emit_baseline counts 120 verdicts, double-counting the first 60 mutants — detection_rate is corrupted
Harness re-run with no dedup silently inflates `detected` count, causing a false 'meets_threshold=true' for a class
findings.suggested_fix: At harness startup: `done = {json.loads(l)['pair_id'] for l in open('e2_verdicts.jsonl') if os.path.exists('e2_verdicts.jsonl') else []}` then `if pair_id in done: continue` in the main loop. Add an assertion in the AC proving command: `pair_ids=[json.loads(l)['pair_id'] for l in open('runs/e2_verdicts.jsonl')]; assert len(pair_ids)==len(set(pair_ids)), 'duplicate pair_ids'`.

findings.finding: The crash-resume mechanism (`e2_verdicts.jsonl` is 'journaled incrementally') does not specify how duplicate rows are prevented on resume. If a run crashes mid-batch and is restarted, pairs that were already processed and appended to the JSONL will be re-run and appended again, causing `e2_baseline.json` to be computed over duplicate rows — silently inflating detected/FP counts and invalidating the baseline.
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py entry ('journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)')
findings.suggested_fix: On harness startup: load the set of already-processed `pair_id`s from `e2_verdicts.jsonl` (if it exists); skip any pair whose `pair_id` is already in that set. Recreate the tmp clone fresh on every (re)start (a `git clone --local` of a local repo is fast) to avoid doubly-mutated ticket state; the skip-set ensures already-journaled pairs are not re-run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a cleanup path for the tmp mutant store in `run_e2_benchmark.py` (e.g., `shutil.rmtree(TMP_MUTANT_STORE)` in a `finally` block, or a `--keep-tmp` flag for debugging), and add a note in the ## Scope section documenting this retirement behavior.
evidence: 'The harness therefore creates the mutant store with `git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>`' — creation specified
No cleanup, deletion, or `finally` block mentioned anywhere in the plan or ACs
The isolation AC only checks that HEAD is unchanged after a run, not that the tmp directory is removed
T9 checklist: 'There is a RETIRE/cleanup path — it doesn't leak/accumulate'
findings.finding: The tmp mutant store (a standalone git clone created in a tmp directory) has no specified cleanup/retirement path in the plan: the harness creates the clone but the plan does not say when or how it is deleted, nor does the AC verify its removal — git clones of a tickets branch with history can be tens to hundreds of MB, and repeated benchmark runs (or aborted runs) will accumulate undeleted clones.
findings.location: ## What — Mutant store paragraph; ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py`
scenarios: CI runner runs the benchmark 10 times (development iterations); each leaves a multi-hundred-MB git clone in /tmp; disk fills up and CI fails
An aborted run (Ctrl-C) leaves the clone on disk; the next run creates another clone at a different tmp path; both persist
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the harness body in `try/finally: shutil.rmtree(TMP_MUTANT_STORE, ignore_errors=True)` so the clone is deleted on both normal exit and crash; add `--keep-tmp` flag to suppress deletion for debugging.

findings.finding: The plan says advisory findings count for detection ('advisory counts, because the proposed criteria ship advisory-first') but the live gate splits advisory findings into `advisory` (within the advisory cap) and `overflow` (beyond the cap, sidecar-only). The plan never specifies whether an `overflow` finding — which has `decision='advisory'` but was demoted past the cap — counts as detected. This is a co-located schema/decision-rule gap: the processing protocol (advisory counts) is stated without reference to the advisory-cap split that determines which advisory findings are accessible.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; 'detected (mutant, recall)' rule
findings.suggested_fix: Given the plan's advisory-first intent, state explicitly: 'overflow findings (advisory findings beyond the cap) also count as detected — `classify_pair` scans `blocking + advisory + overflow + indeterminate` for target-family findings with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}`'. Update the golden tests to include an overflow-advisory fixture.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--verify-clean` to the `assemble_e2_mutants.py` scope description, specifying that it cross-checks every selected base ticket's `outcome_corpus.jsonl` row against the 5-condition filter, exits 0 if all pass, and names the first violating ticket (with its corpus row values) on non-zero exit.
evidence: Scope: '`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing' — only flag described
AC2 proving command: `python3 docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean && echo FILTER-OK`

AC2: '(exits 0 only if every selected base ticket's corpus row passes the 5-condition filter; non-zero names the first violating ticket)' — this is the only behavioral description of `--verify-clean`, buried in the AC rather than the Scope.
findings.finding: The `--verify-clean` flag referenced in AC2's proving command (`assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean`) is not described in the Scope section for `assemble_e2_mutants.py`. The scope says the script's `--dry-run` 'prints the selected base pool without writing,' but never mentions `--verify-clean` or its behavior (cross-check each base against its corpus row). An executor implementing the script has no specification for this flag's semantics.
findings.location: AC: 'The cleanly-closed filter is implemented in `assemble_e2_mutants.py`...' / Scope: `assemble_e2_mutants.py`
scenarios: Executor reads only the Scope section and implements `assemble_e2_mutants.py` without `--verify-clean`; the proving command fails at the flag-parsing level before any filter logic runs.

Executor infers the behavior from the AC parenthetical but must make judgment calls about output format, exit-code semantics for partial failures, and whether `--verify-clean` implies a live `rebar show` check or only corpus-row checks.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `assemble_e2_mutants.py`, append: '`--verify-clean` (combinable with `--dry-run`): for each base ticket selected by the pool filter, re-reads its `outcome_corpus.jsonl` row and asserts all five cleanly-closed conditions; exits non-zero and prints the first violating `ticket_id` + failing field if any row fails.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the tmp mutant store location strategy in the Scope description for `run_e2_benchmark.py` — e.g., 'use `tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="e2_mutant_store_")` cleaned up via `shutil.rmtree` in a `try/finally` block; expose `--tmp-dir` to override for debugging' — so the executor does not make an unguided design choice.
evidence: Plan: 'the harness therefore creates the mutant store with `git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>`' — `TMP_MUTANT_STORE` is a placeholder with no runtime binding specified
Plan: 'REBAR_TRACKER_DIR = TMP_MUTANT_STORE' — same placeholder used in env-var assignment
Scope section for `run_e2_benchmark.py` does not mention a `--tmp-dir` CLI arg or `tempfile.mkdtemp()`
findings.finding: The tmp mutant store's lifecycle (creation location and cleanup) is unspecified. The plan says the harness creates a `git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>` but never states how `TMP_MUTANT_STORE` is determined at runtime (e.g., `tempfile.mkdtemp()`, a CLI `--tmp-dir` argument, a fixed sibling path) or whether/when it is cleaned up after the run. This is a defaultable gap but leaves the executor making an unguided design choice that affects reproducibility and disk usage.
findings.location: ## What — 'Mutant store = an INDEPENDENT git clone...' / Scope: `run_e2_benchmark.py`
scenarios: Executor uses a fixed sibling path (e.g., `.tickets-tracker-e2-tmp`) that persists across runs and could accumulate stale state, causing the crash-resume logic to read a partially-mutated store from a prior run.
Executor uses `tempfile.mkdtemp()` but never cleans it up, leaving large git clones on disk; or cleans it on success but not on failure, complicating debugging.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` scope bullet: 'The tmp mutant store is created under `tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="e2_mutant_")` and removed in a `try/finally` block; a `--keep-tmp` flag suppresses deletion for post-run inspection. A `--tmp-dir PATH` override allows repeated runs against the same clone for crash-resume.`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the file-open semantics for `e2_verdicts.jsonl` in `run_e2_benchmark.py`: define whether a fresh run truncates the file first (recommended) or appends, and add an assertion in the AC proving command that the number of verdict rows equals the corpus size (no duplicates, no stale rows).
evidence: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' — mode of file open (append vs. overwrite) not specified
The AC proving command iterates all lines of e2_verdicts.jsonl without asserting uniqueness or line count vs. corpus size
T9 checklist: 'Update concurrency / ownership is clear' and 'Consumers enumerated and tolerant of absence/staleness'
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `e2_verdicts.jsonl` is overwritten or appended on a fresh (non-resuming) re-run: if the file is appended to, a deliberate full re-run (e.g., after fixing a gate bug) would produce a file mixing old and new verdicts, causing `emit_baseline` to count both — silently producing a corrupted baseline that passes the validity guard.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py`; AC: BASELINE-OK proving command
scenarios: Developer re-runs the full benchmark after fixing a gate bug; the harness appends to the existing file; emit_baseline counts 200 rows instead of 100; detection_rate is wrong
A stale e2_verdicts.jsonl from a previous corpus version mixes with new rows, producing cross-version contamination
findings.suggested_fix: Open `e2_verdicts.jsonl` in write mode (`'w'`) at the start of a full run, and in append mode (`'a'`) only when resuming (detected by the presence of already-completed pair_ids); document this in the ## Scope description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit exit-code checks and error surfacing for all subprocess calls (`git clone`, `rebar edit`) in `run_e2_benchmark.py`, and after each `rebar edit` injection, validate that the mutant's stored plan text contains the expected mutation diff before proceeding.
evidence: 'creates the mutant store with `git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>`' — no failure handling stated
'injects each seeded defect into the clone via `rebar edit <id> --description=…`' — no error handling stated
'The harness ASSERTS the clone is standalone … BEFORE any injection' — asserts structure but not injection success
No mention of checking rebar edit exit codes or validating mutant content post-injection
findings.finding: The plan specifies `git clone --local --branch tickets` and `rebar edit <id> --description=…` as subprocess operations but provides no error handling for these external calls failing (e.g., the tickets branch not existing in the clone, a `rebar edit` failing for a malformed description, or disk exhaustion during clone); a silent failure in mutation injection would leave the mutant store with an un-mutated plan, causing the harness to score a control verdict against a mutant row — corrupting the labeled corpus without any error.
findings.location: ## What — Mutant store paragraph; ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py`
scenarios: rebar edit fails (non-zero exit) for one mutant but the harness continues; that pair's 'mutant' is actually the un-mutated control plan; the gate scores it as a control-level verdict, inflating FN count
git clone fails due to missing tickets branch; harness crashes without a clear error message, leaving tmp dirs on disk

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/experiments/__init__.py` to the scope list (can be empty, matching the convention of all other `tests/unit/` subdirectories) to ensure pytest discovers the new golden tests in the standard `pytest tests/` run.
evidence: All existing tests/unit/ subdirs verified to have __init__.py: grounding/__init__.py (empty), workflow/__init__.py (empty), rebar_reconciler/ has __init__.py files
Plan scope list does not include `tests/unit/experiments/__init__.py`
pyproject.toml: addopts = '--strict-markers --strict-config'; testpaths = ['tests']
findings.finding: The plan scopes two new test files at `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` and `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_benchmark.py`, creating a new `tests/unit/experiments/` subdirectory. Every existing subdirectory of `tests/unit/` (`grounding/`, `workflow/`, `rebar_reconciler/`, `io/`, etc.) contains an `__init__.py`. The plan's scope list does not include `tests/unit/experiments/__init__.py`. Under pytest's default `--import-mode=prepend` (and the project's `rootdir`-based discovery), an `__init__.py`-less subdirectory may fail to be collected or may collide with same-named modules in other dirs.
findings.location: ## Scope — test files: `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py`, `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_benchmark.py`
scenarios: CI runs `pytest tests/unit/experiments/` and pytest fails to collect the tests or raises an ImportError because the new subdirectory lacks an __init__.py, silently omitting the golden tests from the CI suite.
The AC prove commands `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py -q` pass locally but the tests are not collected in the project's default `pytest tests/` run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/experiments/__init__.py` (empty file) to the ## Scope list, consistent with `tests/unit/grounding/__init__.py` and `tests/unit/workflow/__init__.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the hardcoded `--branch tickets` in the `git clone` command with the dynamically resolved branch name from `rebar.config.tickets_branch(REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)` so the harness is portable to repos with a custom tracker branch name.
evidence: Plan text: '`git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>`'
src/rebar/config.py:139-149: `tickets_branch()` reads `load_config(root).tracker.branch`, defaulting to `'tickets'`
The same configurable branch name is used in `_commands/init.py:342` (`tickets_branch(repo)`) for worktree creation
findings.finding: The harness description specifies `git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT> <TMP_MUTANT_STORE>` with the branch name hardcoded as `tickets`. The rebar codebase resolves the tickets branch name through `rebar.config.tickets_branch()` (config.py:139-149), which respects `tracker.branch` config key / `REBAR_TRACKER_BRANCH` env var, defaulting to `tickets`. A repo with a custom tickets branch name would cause the clone to fail (branch not found), or silently clone a different branch and make the mutant store incoherent.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section, standalone clone paragraph; ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description
scenarios: A team that configured `tracker.branch = 'ticket-events'` (to avoid collision with an existing `tickets` branch) runs the harness and gets `fatal: Remote branch tickets not found in upstream origin`, causing the benchmark to abort before any gate run.
The harness clones branch `tickets` which does not match the configured branch, silently producing an empty or wrong mutant store, leading to garbage baseline results.
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`, resolve the branch name via `import rebar.config; branch = rebar.config.tickets_branch(REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)` and use `git clone --local --branch {branch} ...` rather than hardcoding `tickets`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of rationale to the counting rule explaining why advisory findings count for detection: 'The proposed R-criteria (R1/R3/R4) ship advisory-first by design; counting only block-decision findings would systematically miss true-positive detections until those criteria are promoted to blocking, making the benchmark useless for evaluating advisory-first detectors.'
evidence: 'detected (mutant, recall): a target-family finding surfaced with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}` — advisory counts, because the proposed criteria ship advisory-first'

The justification phrase 'because the proposed criteria ship advisory-first' appears once but the logical chain (advisory-first criteria → advisory findings ARE the detection signal → blocking-only counting would systematically under-count) is not spelled out
The e2_counting.py golden test for classify_pair must cover this branch, but the rationale for WHY advisory counts (not just THAT it counts) is absent from the plan
findings.finding: The plan's positive rationale for the counting rule choice ('advisory counts for detection, only `block` counts for blocking-FP') is stated as a rule without explaining why this asymmetry is the right calibration choice. Since the proposed R-criteria ship advisory-first, counting advisory findings for detection is load-bearing — an implementer who misconfigures the counting helper (e.g., using block-only for detection) would produce systematically lower recall numbers that would prevent R1/R3/R4 from clearing their thresholds even with a correctly-functioning detector.
findings.location: ## What — 'AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling (the counting rule, pinned)' section
scenarios: An implementer writing classify_pair sees the block/advisory asymmetry and, absent rationale, treats advisory as 'weak signal' and implements block-only detection — the test fixtures catch this, but only if the tests are written correctly; unclear rationale increases the risk of both the implementation and the test having the same bug

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `target_criteria` key per class to `runs/e2_thresholds.json` (alongside `detection_min`/`blocking_fp_max`) so `classify_pair` reads the attribution set from config rather than hardcoding it in `e2_counting.py`, and update the AC proof command to assert those keys are present.
evidence: Plan table lists 'Current-gate nearest criteria (baseline)' per class (E4, G1G2, G6, G3, G4, A1) but does not state these are stored in e2_thresholds.json or any other config file.
e2_thresholds.json schema (AC proof): `{detection_min, blocking_fp_max}` per class + `indeterminate_cap` — no `target_criteria` key mentioned.
Plan: 'each validated against the live registry (plan_review.registry.by_id) at run time' — validation is runtime but the list itself must be hardcoded somewhere in the harness or e2_counting.py.
R1/R3/R4/R2 each add new criteria IDs; the plan says they re-run this harness — implying the harness's target_criteria must be updated each time a new criterion lands.
findings.finding: The per-class `target_criteria` sets used in `classify_pair` (e.g., `['E4','G1G2','G6']` for class 1, `['G3','G4']` for classes 2/3) appear to be hardcoded inside `e2_counting.py` or the harness, rather than read from the configurable `runs/e2_thresholds.json`. Because R1, R3, R4, and R2 each add new criterion IDs that must be attributed to their respective defect classes, updating the attribution requires a code change to `e2_counting.py` (a test-covered module with golden fixtures) rather than a config-file edit — adding friction and a risk of attribution silently lagging the live gate.
findings.location: ## What — 'AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling' section; `e2_counting.py` (Scope); per-class `target_criteria` column in the baseline table
scenarios: R1 lands and adds criterion 'A9' for asserted-capability detection; the E2 harness still attributes class-1 only to ['E4','G1G2','G6'] — the new criterion's findings are not counted toward class-1 detection, silently under-reporting R1's detection rate.

A criterion is renamed in the registry; the live-registry validation at runtime raises an error, but without a config location the fix requires editing the test-covered e2_counting.py and regenerating golden fixtures.
findings.suggested_fix: In `runs/e2_thresholds.json`, add `"target_criteria": ["E4", "G1G2", "G6"]` (etc.) per class. In `e2_counting.py`, accept `target_criteria` as a parameter (already is) but have `run_e2_benchmark.py` load it from the thresholds JSON rather than a hardcoded constant. Update the AC schema proof to assert the key exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add rate-limit backoff/retry (e.g., exponential backoff on 429/529 responses) and specify the deduplication logic for crash-resumption in `run_e2_benchmark.py` (e.g., skip `pair_id` rows already present in `e2_verdicts.jsonl` on re-run).
evidence: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' — implies sequential batch of LLM calls
'≥10 MUTANT rows for each of the 5 defect classes … each paired with exactly one CONTROL row' → ≥100 gate calls minimum
No rate-limit, backoff, or retry language anywhere in the plan
T5a checklist: 'Input-size limits, concurrency/rate-limit/pool handling, and load expectations stated'
findings.finding: The harness issues ≥100 sequential production-gate calls (each invoking the Anthropic LLM API) with no rate-limit handling, backoff, or retry specified; a burst of 100+ API calls can hit Anthropic's TPM/RPM limits, causing the run to fail mid-batch and, since the deduplication logic for crash-resumption is unspecified, potentially leaving the corpus in an ambiguous partially-processed state.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py`; ## What — gate-execution mechanism paragraph
scenarios: Harness issues 50 calls, hits Anthropic RPM limit, crashes — resumes but deduplication logic is unspecified so it may re-run completed rows or skip them
Anthropic returns a 529 (overloaded) error; harness has no retry and exits non-zero, requiring full manual restart
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`: (1) wrap each `review_plan(...)` call with a retry loop (3 attempts, exponential backoff starting at 5 s) on Anthropic rate-limit/overload exceptions; (2) at startup, load already-completed `pair_id` values from `e2_verdicts.jsonl` into a set and skip them — this makes resumption idempotent and safe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the POSIX shell prove commands in the threshold-preregistration and isolation ACs (and any other shell-syntax ACs) with a cross-platform alternative — e.g., a `python3 -c` one-liner or a dedicated `verify_e2_acs.py` script — so they are executable on Windows CI/developer workstations as well as POSIX.
evidence: Plan AC text: `TH=$(git log --diff-filter=A --format=%H -1 -- …); BL=$(git log …); [ "$TH" != "$BL" ] && git merge-base --is-ancestor "$TH" "$BL" && echo PRE-REGISTERED-OK`
Plan AC text: `H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); .venv/bin/python …; H1=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); [ "$H0" = "$H1" ] && echo ISOLATION-OK`
Windows is an explicitly listed platform in the rebar support matrix; cmd.exe has no `$()` command substitution or POSIX `[ ]` test builtin.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria prove commands use POSIX shell syntax (command substitution `$(…)`, bracket-test `[ "$X" = "$Y" ]`, `&&`-chaining) that is unavailable in cmd.exe; a developer or CI agent running verification on Windows would get immediate syntax errors and be unable to confirm any AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Pre-registered thresholds' AC (`TH=$(git log …); BL=$(git log …); [ "$TH" != "$BL" ] && git merge-base --is-ancestor "$TH" "$BL" && echo PRE-REGISTERED-OK`) and 'Isolation' AC (`H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); …; [ "$H0" = "$H1" ] && echo ISOLATION-OK`)
scenarios: Windows CI or developer workstation (Windows platform cell): running the threshold-preregistration or isolation prove commands in cmd.exe produces `'TH' is not recognized` and `'[' is not recognized as an internal or external command`, meaning the AC cannot be verified and the prove step silently or noisily fails — the developer cannot confirm pre-registration order or store isolation.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the shell variable comparisons into a small Python helper: `python3 docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/verify_e2_acs.py --check pre-registered` and `--check isolation`, which internally calls `subprocess.run(['git','log',…])` and compares outputs — works identically on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `plan_review.registry.by_id` capability assertion with the actual API: use `registry.CANONICAL_LLM | set(registry.CANONICAL_DET)` (or `set(registry.load_criteria().keys())` / `registry.effective_routing().keys()`) to validate that each criterion ID in the baseline table is a known registry member at run time.
evidence: Plan text: 'each validated against the live registry (`plan_review.registry.by_id`) at run time so no non-existent id is asserted'

src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py: no `by_id` symbol exists — the module exports load_criteria(), effective_routing(), CANONICAL_LLM (frozenset), AGENT_TIER (frozenset), CODEBASE_GROUNDED (frozenset), check_registry_coverage(), and overlay helpers, none of which is a `by_id` dict
ABSENCE: `search_files('by_id', path='src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py')` returns no matches
findings.finding: The plan asserts criteria IDs are 'validated against the live registry (`plan_review.registry.by_id`) at run time', but `by_id` does not exist as an attribute of `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py` — the module exports `load_criteria()`, `effective_routing()`, `CANONICAL_LLM` (a frozenset), `AGENT_TIER`, and related functions, with no `by_id` mapping. Any harness code that uses `plan_review.registry.by_id` will raise `AttributeError` at run time.
findings.location: ## What — current-gate baseline table, column 'Current-gate nearest criteria (baseline)': 'each validated against the live registry (`plan_review.registry.by_id`) at run time so no non-existent id is asserted'
scenarios: The harness calls `plan_review.registry.by_id[criterion_id]` to validate that the criterion IDs in the baseline table exist in the production registry; this raises AttributeError at import or call time, breaking the run before any gate call is made.
If the harness author treats the plan's assertion as authoritative and writes `registry.by_id`, the CI-collectable tests will fail with an import or attribute error.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the plan text's reference to `plan_review.registry.by_id` with `plan_review.registry.CANONICAL_LLM` (a frozenset of all LLM-tier IDs) or `set(plan_review.registry.load_criteria().keys())` (all merged IDs including project overlays). For example: `assert all(cid in plan_review.registry.CANONICAL_LLM or cid in plan_review.registry.CANONICAL_DET for cid in target_criteria), f'unknown criterion id in {target_criteria}'`.

findings.finding: `classify_pair`'s definition references `verdict_dict['findings']` — a key that does not exist in the actual `review_plan` return dict, which has `blocking`, `advisory`, `coaching`, `overflow`, `indeterminate`, `dropped` but NO `'findings'` top-level key (confirmed in orchestrator.py:531-544). An implementer following the plan as written will get a `KeyError` or empty result on every run.
findings.location: ## What — AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling section; `classify_pair` definition
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `verdict_dict['findings']` reference with explicit iteration over `verdict_dict.get('blocking', []) + verdict_dict.get('advisory', []) + verdict_dict.get('indeterminate', []) + verdict_dict.get('dropped', []) + verdict_dict.get('overflow', [])`, and clarify in the counting rule whether overflow-capped advisory findings count as detected (since advisory-first discipline says advisory counts).

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules' to justify that class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) code-grounding is real. But passes.py:269 is `prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (prompt-template resolution) and passes.py:514 is `prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', ...)` (an ISF-summarizer prompt fetch). Neither is an agentic code-grounding call. The actual mechanism by which agentic criteria (E4, G1G2, A1, G6 in the AGENT_TIER frozenset) read the real code is through the workflow's RunnerAgentStep / gate_dispatch.py's agent_runner — not these specific pass lines.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism paragraph: '`source="local"` sets no code ContextVar, so `ctx.repo_root = resolve_code_root(repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)` and `cfg.repo_path` = the real checkout; the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) and the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical with the correct mechanism citations: '…the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) and the agentic criteria (CODEBASE_GROUNDED: E4/G1G2/A1/G6) read code via `cfg.repo_path` = REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT (set by `apply_handle` in `gate_source.py:120-126` for local mode, or via `RunnerAgentStep(repo_root=repo_root, config=cfg)` in `gate_dispatch.py:169`).'

findings.finding: The AC1 proving command does not assert the class-5 minimum count of ≥5; it only asserts that class '5' exists in the Counter (via `set(m)=={'1','2','3','4','5'}`), while `all(m[k]>=10 for k in '1234')` iterates only characters '1','2','3','4', silently skipping the ≥5 floor for class 5. The AC text claims 'the proving command asserts ≥10 for classes 1–4 and the recorded N for class 5' but no such assertion for the recorded N of class 5 is present in the command.
findings.location: AC1 proving command (the `python3 -c` one-liner)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the assert to: `assert all(m[k]>=10 for k in '1234') and m['5']>=5, (dict(m),dict(ctl))`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
